extra.reasoning:
The property requires that globally (opted_out implies not sent). The only transition that sets sent:=true while opted_out is true is [t5] (on send_attempt if opted_out and ticks >= 12). So the violation hinges on reaching a state with opted_out=true and ticks>=12, then issuing send_attempt.

Reachability: ticks only increases via [t4] (tick), capped at 12; tick does not affect opted_out. [t1] (optout) sets opted_out:=true without touching ticks. Therefore the state opted_out=true, ticks=12 is reachable: fire optout once (opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0), then fire tick 12 times (ticks climbs to 12, opted_out remains true, sent remains false because t4 also sets sent:=false each tick).

From state (opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=12), a send_attempt event enables both [t3] (sent:=false) and [t5] (sent:=true). Since the property must hold on EVERY reachable execution and [t5] is an enabled transition that can fire, the execution where t5 fires produces opted_out=true, sent=true, directly violating opted_out implies not sent. (Even under a more-specific-guard priority, t5 is the more specific rule and would be selected; under nondeterminism it is among the enabled choices.)

The counterexample uses 1 optout + 12 tick + 1 send_attempt = 14 events, within the 16-event bound. After the final send_attempt, sent=true and opted_out=true, which is the violating state.
